elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce the concept of cyclicity and hypercyclicity in self-similar groups as an analogue of cyclic and hypercyclic vectors for an ope-rator on a Banach space.
elements.sent-2411-002: We derive a sufficient condition for cyclicity of non-finitary automorphisms in contracting discrete automata groups.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the profinite setting we prove that fractal profinite groups may be regarded as measure-preserving dynamical systems and derive a sufficient condition for the ergodicity and the mixing properties of these dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, we show that a Haar-random element in a super strongly fractal profinite group is hypercyclic almost surely as an application of Birkhoff's ergodic theorem for free semigroup actions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Self-similar groups are strongly connected to dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: A great example of this connection are the iterated monodromy groups of complex polynomials, which were used by Bartholdi and Nekrashevych to answer a well-known open problem in complex dynamics \cite{Thurston .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Here our aim is to establish a connection between self-similar profinite groups and measure-preserving dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: We start by regarding a profinite group~$G$ as a probability space via its unique Haar measure $\mu_G$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Assume further that $G$ is a self-similar subgroup of $\mathrm{Aut ~T$ for some regular rooted tree $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Then we may regard~$T$ as a free monoid and define an action $\mathcal{T}$ of $T$ on $(G,\mu_G)$ by considering sections, i.e. each $v\in T$ yields an operator $\mathcal{T _v$ given by $\mathcal{T _v(g):=g|_v$ for every $g\in G$; see \cref{section: Preliminaries for the unexplained terms here and elsewhere in the introduction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: A natural question here is whether this action $\mathcal{T}$ of $T$ on $(G,\mu_G)$ is measure-preserving.
elements.sent-2411-012: If so, then the tuple $(G,\mu_G,\mathcal{T ,T)$ becomes a measure-preserving dynamical system and we are further interested on the ergodicity and the mixing properties of this dynamical system.}
elements.sent-2411-013: We obtain the following: \begin{Theorem Let $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ be a self-similar profinite group:.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These orbits are closely related to the invariant subsets for a measure-preserving transformation.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the case of a single operator acting on a Banach space these invariant subspaces have been thoroughly studied and continue to be an active area of research in functional analysis, see \cite{Book, Enflo1, Lomonosov, Read .}
elements.sent-2411-016: General invariant subspaces are often studied via the so-called cyclic and hypercyclic vectors, i.e. those vectors which are not contained in a proper invariant subspace or subset respectively; see \cite{Cyclicity .}
elements.sent-2411-017: We define the analogue of cyclic and hypercyclic vectors for self-similar groups in terms of the natural action via sections of the free monoid~$T$ on $(G,\mu_G)$; see \cref{section: example for precise definitions.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The following questions arise naturally: \begin{Question Does every fractal profinite group contain a cyclic automorphism? And a hypercyclic automorphism? \end{Question By \cref{Theorem: fractal and ergodic a super strongly fractal profinite group may be regarded as an ergodic measure-preserving dynamical system and therefore we may apply the machinery in ergodic theory to study Haar-random elements.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Indeed, we shall make use of a generalized version of Birkhoff's theorem for free semigroup actions to prove the following: \begin{Theorem Let $G$ be a super strongly fractal profinite group.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Then a Haar-random element of~$G$ is hypercyclic with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-021: In fact, Abért and Virág proved in \cite[Theorem~7.2]{AbertVirag that three random elements in the pro-$p$ Sylow subgroup $W_p$ of the automorphism group of the $p$-adic tree generate topologically a closed subgroup of Hausdorff dimension 1 in $W_p$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus by a recent result of the author \cite[Theorem~1.2]{JorgeSpectra , 3-random generated subgroups of $W_p$ cannot be self-similar.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Lastly, \cref{Theorem: fractal and ergodic also opens the door to applying methods from dynamical systems in the study of self-similar groups.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The author together with Radi have recently utilized \cref{Theorem: fractal and ergodic in \cite{JorgeSanti to obtain new results on the fixed-point proportion of super strongly fractal groups, which itself has a wide range of applications in arithmetic dynamics; compare \cite{FPP1,FFP3,FFP2,FFP4 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cref{section: Preliminaries we introduce the background material needed in the subsequent sections.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Cyclicity and hypercyclicity in self-similar groups are introduced in \cref{section: example via a motivating example which is both an $\mathbb{F _p$-vector space and a self-similar group.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: We further study cyclic automorphisms in discrete automata groups to conclude \cref{section: example .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally in \cref{section: fractal groups we prove \textcolor{teal {Theorems \ref{Theorem: fractal and ergodic and \ref{Theorem: super strongly fractal cyclic showing an application of Birkhoff's ergodic theorem for free semigroup actions to the theory of self-similar profinite groups.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: An event in a probability space will be said to happen almost surely if it happens with probability 1.
elements.sent-2411-030: We denote the finite field of $p$ elements by $\mathbb{F _p$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Self-similar groups will be assumed to act on the tree on the right so composition will be written from left to right.
elements.sent-2411-032: We shall use exponential notation for group actions on the tree.
elements.sent-2411-033: Finally, we denote by $\# S$ the cardinality of a finite set $S$.
elements.sent-2411-034: I would like to thank my advisors Gustavo A.
elements.sent-2411-035: Fernández-Alcober and Anitha Thillaisundaram for their valuable feedback which contributed greatly to the final version of this paper.
elements.subsection-2411-003: The Haar measure on profinite groups
elements.sent-2411-037: We define the Borel algebra of $G$ as the $\sigma$-algebra generated by all the open subsets of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The elements of the Borel algebra of $G$ will be called Borel sets.
elements.sent-2411-039: A $\pi$-system on $G$ is a non-empty collection $\mathcal{A}$ of subsets of $G$ closed under finite intersections, i.e. if $A,B\in \mathcal{A}$ then $A\cap B\in \mathcal{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, a family of neighborhood bases for each point in $G$ forms a $\pi$-system.
elements.sent-2411-041: The $\sigma$-algebra generated by such a $\pi$-system is precisely the Borel algebra of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We recall the following standard fact about $\pi$-systems: \begin{fact Let $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ be two probability measures on a set $X$ and let $P$ be a $\pi$-system on $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: If for every $A\in P$ we have $\mu_1(A)=\mu_2(A)$, then $\mu_1(S)=\mu_2(S)$ for any set $S$ in the $\sigma$-algebra generated by the $\pi$-system $P$.
elements.sent-2411-044: By Haar's theorem a profinite group $G$ admits a unique Haar measure, which we shall denote by $\mu_G$.
elements.sent-2411-045: From \cref{fact: pi systems and both the left-translation invariance and the uniqueness of the Haar measure the following is immediate:.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Let $\mu$ be a measure on the Borel algebra in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-047: If $\mu$ coincides with the Haar measure $\mu_G$ on a neighborhood basis of the identity in $G$, then $\mu=\mu_G$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Note that for an open subgroup $H\le G$ we have $\mu_G(H)=|G:H|^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Let $(X,\mu)$ be a probability space and let $A$ be a monoid.
elements.sent-2411-050: We fix an action $\mathcal{T}$ of $A$ on $(X,\mu)$ and we say this monoid action is measure-preserving if for any measurable subset $S\subseteq X$ and any $a\in A$ we have $\mu(\mathcal{T _a^{-1 (S))=\mu(S)$, where $\mathcal{T _a$ is the operator associated to the action of $a$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Then the tuple $(X,\mu,\mathcal{T ,A)$ is called a measure-preserving dynamical system.}
elements.sent-2411-052: A measurable subset $S\subseteq X$ such that $\mathcal{T _a^{-1 (S)= S$ for every $a\in A$ is called $\mathcal{T$-invariant .}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We say that a measure-preserving dynamical system $(X,\mu,\mathcal{T ,A)$ is ergodic if for every $\mathcal{T}$-invariant measurable subset $S\subseteq X$ we have $\mu(S)\in \{0,1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: A stronger notion in ergodic theory is that of strong mixing.
elements.sent-2411-055: We adapt the usual definition for a single operator to the action of a free monoid $F$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We define a dynamical system $(X,\mu,\mathcal{T ,F)$ to be strongly mixing if for any pair $S_1,S_2$ of measurable subsets and any sequence $\{a_k\}_{k\ge 1}\subseteq F\setminus\{1\}$ the following limit exists: \begin{equation}\lim_{k\to\infty}\mu(T_{a_1\dotsb a_k}^{-1}(S_1)\cap S_2)=\mu(S_1)\cdot \mu(S_2).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-057: For a $\mathcal{T}$-invariant measurable subset $S$ the strong mixing condition implies that $\mu(S)=\mu(S)^2$, i.e. $\mu(S)\in \{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: If a measure-preserving dynamical system $(X,\mu,\mathcal{T ,F)$ is strongly mixing then it is ergodic.}
elements.sent-2411-060: For a natural number $m\ge 1$ and a finite set of $m$ symbols $\{1,\dotsc,m\}$, we define the free monoid on $m$ generators as the monoid consisting of finite words with letters in $\{1,\dotsc,m\}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The free monoid can be identified with the $m$-adic tree, i.e. the rooted tree $T$ where each vertex has exactly $m$ immediate descendants.
elements.sent-2411-062: The words in~$T$ of length exactly $n$ form the $n$th level of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-063: We may also use the term level to refer to the number $n$.
elements.sent-2411-064: For a vertex $v\in T$ we write $|v|$ for the length of $v$ as a word with letters in $\{1,\dotsc,m\}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $\mathrm{Aut~ T$ be the group of graph automorphisms of the $m$-adic tree $T$, i.e. those bijective maps on the set of vertices of $T$ preserving adjacency.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Such automorphisms fix the root and permute the vertices at the same level of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-067: For any natural number $n\ge 1$, the $n$th truncated tree $T_n$ consists of the vertices at distance at most $n$ from the root.
elements.sent-2411-068: We denote the group of automorphisms of the $n$th truncated tree by $\mathrm{Aut~ T_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: For $1\le n\le \infty$ we define the section of $g$ at $v$ of depth $n$ as the unique automorphism $g|_v^n\in\mathrm{Aut~ T_n$ such that $(vu)^g=v^gu^{g|_v^n}$ for every $u$ of length at most $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: For $n=\infty$ we write $g|_v$ for simplicity and call it the section of $g$ at $v$, while for $n=1$ we shall call $g|_v^1$ the label of $g$ at $v$.
elements.sent-2411-072: For every pair $g,h\in \mathrm{Aut ~T$, $v\in T$ and $n\ge 1$ we have the equality \begin{equation}(gh)|_v^n=(g|_v^n)(h|_{v^g}^n).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For every $n\ge 1$ the subset $\mathrm{St (n)$ of automorphisms fixing all the vertices of the $n$th level of $T$ is a normal subgroup of finite index in $\mathrm{Aut~ T$, the $n$th level stabilizer.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Similarly, for any vertex $v\in T$ we define its vertex stabilizer $\mathrm{st (v)$ as the subgroup of automorphisms fixing the vertex $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The group $\mathrm{Aut~ T$ is a countably based profinite group with respect to the topology induced by its action on each level of the tree.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We call this topology the congruence topology.
elements.sent-2411-077: For each vertex~$v$ we shall define the continuous homomorphisms $\varphi_v:\mathrm{st (v)\to \mathrm{Aut~ T$ given by $g\mapsto g|_v$ and, more generally, for every $1\le n\le \infty$ we define $\varphi_v^n:\mathrm{st (v)\to \mathrm{Aut ~T_n$ given by $g\mapsto g|_v^n$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We further define the continuous isomorphism $\psi:\mathrm{Aut ~T\to \big(\mathrm{Aut~ T\times\overset{m {\ldots}\times \mathrm{Aut~ T\big)\rtimes \mathrm{Sym (m)$ given by $g\mapsto (g|_{1 ,\dotsc,g|_m)g|_\emptyset^1$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We define finitary automorphisms of depth $n$ as those automorphisms of the $m$-adic tree with trivial labels at the $k$th level of the tree for every $k\ge n$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Finitary automorphisms of depth 1 are called rooted automorphisms and they may be identified with elements in the symmetric group $\mathrm{Sym (m)$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: More generally, the group of finitary automorphisms of depth $n$ is isomorphic to the group $\mathrm{Aut ~T_n$ of automorphisms of the $n$th truncated tree via the projection of the action of $\mathrm{Aut ~T$ to the $n$th truncated tree $T_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: For a subgroup $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ we define $\mathrm{st _G(v):=\mathrm{st (v)\cap G$ and $\mathrm{St _G(n):=\mathrm{St (n)\cap G$ for any vertex $v$ and any level $n\ge 1$ respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The quotients $G_n:=G/\mathrm{St _G(n)$ are called the congruence quotients of $G$ and their elements the $n$-patterns of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We say that $G$ is self-similar if for any $g\in G$ and any vertex $v\in T$ we have $g|_{v}\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-086: We shall say that $G$ is fractal if $G$ is self-similar and $\varphi_v(\mathrm{st _G(v))=G$ for any $v\in T$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: A stronger version of fractality is that of super strongly fractal groups, where $\varphi_v(\mathrm{St _G(n))=G$ for every vertex $v$ at the $n$th level of $T$ for every level $n\ge 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We shall not assume level-transitivity neither for fractal nor for super strongly fractal groups.
elements.sent-2411-089: In this section we motivate the definition of cyclic and hypercyclic automorphisms via an example.
elements.sent-2411-090: We consider an elementary $p$-abelian self-similar profinite group $A$.
elements.sent-2411-091: We shall use the fact that elementary $p$-abelian groups may be regarded as $\mathbb{F _p$-vector spaces to identify $A$ with the $\mathbb{F _p$-vector space $V$ consisting of infinite sequences over $\mathbb{F _p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Then we show that self-similar closed subgroups of $A$ correspond precisely to the $\mathbb{F _p$-vector subspaces of $V$ invariant under the left-shift operator.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Therefore we study the cyclic and hypercyclic vectors of $V$ for the left-shift operator, which motivates the analogous concepts of cyclic and hypercyclic automorphisms in self-similar profinite groups.
elements.sent-2411-094: Furthermore, we study some discrete subspaces of $V$ and motivate the discrete analogue of cyclic automorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-095: Lastly, we give sufficient conditions for discrete automata groups to admit such cyclic automorphisms and apply this criterion to some of the main examples in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-097: We define the elementary $p$-abelian pro-$p$ group $A$ acting on the $p$-adic tree $T$ as \begin{equation}A:=\overline{\langle g_n~:~ n\ge 0\rangle}\le Aut~T,\end{equation} where, for each $n\ge 0$, the automorphism $g_n$ is given by \begin{equation}g_n|^1_v:= \sigma,  if v is at level k= n; 1,  if v is at level k\ne n. \end{equation} Any element $g\in A$ admits a unique factorization of the form \begin{equation}g=\prod_{n\ge 0}g_n^{e_n},\end{equation} where $0\le e_n\le p-1$.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}g_n|^1_v:= \sigma,  if v is at level k= n; 1,  if v is at level k\ne n. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: Note that the group $A$ is simply the profinite group $G_\mathcal{S}\le W_p$ defined in \cite[Section 4]{JorgeSpectra given by the defining sequence $\mathcal{S =\{S_n\}_{n\ge 0}$, where $S_n:=D_{p^n (\sigma)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Furthermore, since $A$ is elementary $p$-abelian, it admits an $\mathbb{F _p$-vector space structure.}
elements.sent-2411-100: We define the $\mathbb{F _p$-vector space $V$ consisting of infinite sequences over $\mathbb{F _p$ with pointwise addition and scalar multiplication.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Note that the vectors in $V$ may be simply regarded as functions $\mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}\to \mathbb{F _p$ and $V$ is a compact topological space with respect to the product topology.}
elements.sent-2411-102: There is an $\mathbb{F _p$-linear homeomorphism $f:A\to V$ given by \begin{equation}f(g)=f\big(\prod_{n\ge 0}g_n^{e_n}\big):=(e_n)_{n\ge 0}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Let us define the left-shift operator $\tau:V\to V$ via \begin{equation}\tau(e_n)_{n\ge 0}:=(e_{n+1})_{n\ge 0}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: We note that considering sections of an element in $A$ at the first level of $T$ coincides with the action of the left-shift operator on $V$ via the $\mathbb{F _p$-homeomorphism~$f$, as shown in the proof of the following lemma:.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The (closed) subgroup $H$ is self-similar if and only if $f(H)$ is a $\tau$-invariant (closed) subspace.
elements.sent-2411-107: Note that a group $G$ is self-similar if and only if for every $g\in G$ and every~$v$ at the first level of the tree $g|_v\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-108: Now note that for $g=\prod_{n\ge 0 g_n^{e_n}\in A$ and for any vertex $v$ at the first level of $T$ we have \begin{equation}g|_v=\prod_{n\ge 0}g_n^{e_{n+1}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Therefore \begin{equation}f(g|_v)=(e_{n+1})_{n\ge 0}=\tau((e_n)_{n\ge 0})=\tau(f(g)),\end{equation} i.e. the following diagram commutes \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd} A \arrow{r}{f} \arrow[swap]{d}{\cdot |_v} V \arrow{d}{\tau} A \arrow{r}{f} V \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} and the equivalence in the statement is now apparent.
elements.sent-2411-110: The same holds replacing ``subgroup$"$ with ``closed subgroup$"$ as $f$ is a homeomorphism.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Cyclicity and hypercyclicity in self-similar profinite groups
elements.sent-2411-111: A vector $v\in V$ is said to be cyclic for $\tau$ if the closed subspace generated by the $\tau$-orbit of $v$ is $V$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Furthermore if the $\tau$-orbit of $v$ itself is dense in $V$ then $v$ is called hypercyclic.
elements.sent-2411-113: In other words, cyclic (hypercyclic) vectors are those non-trivial vectors which are not contained in any proper $\tau$-invariant closed subspace (subset) of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-114: The commutative diagram above suggests that we may use the homeomorphism $f:A\to V$ to obtain analogous definitions for self-similar profinite groups.
elements.sent-2411-115: Let $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ be a self-similar group, where $T$ is a regular rooted tree.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Given a subgroup $H\le G$, we define the self-similar closure of $H$ in $G$, denoted by $H^{\mathrm{SS }$, as the smallest self-similar subgroup of $G$ containing $H$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In other words \begin{equation}H^{SS}:=\bigcap K,\end{equation} where $K$ ranges over all self-similar subgroups of $G$ such that $H\le K\le G$.
elements.sent-2411-118: An explicit construction of the self-similar closure is given by the following straightforward lemma:.
elements.sent-2411-119: For $H\le G$ we have $H^{\mathrm{SS =\langle h|_v~:~ h\in H\text{ and v\in T\rangle$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We say $g\in G$ is cyclic in~$G$ if $\overline{\langle g\rangle^{\mathrm{SS =G$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The automorphism $g$ is called hypercyclic if $\overline{\{g|_v~:~ v\in T\} =G$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: In other words: an automorphism $g\in G$ is cyclic if it is not contained in any non-trivial proper self-similar closed subgroup of $G$ and hypercyclic if it is not contained in a non-trivial proper self-similar closed subset of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Cyclic and hypercyclic automorphisms can be used to deduce properties of the subgroup structure of a self-similar closed subgroup:.
elements.sent-2411-124: A self-similar closed subgroup $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ contains a non-trivial proper self-similar closed subgroup (subset) if and only if $G$ contains a non-trivial non-cyclic (non-hypercyclic) automorphism.}
elements.sent-2411-125: If $G$ contains a non-trivial proper self-similar closed subgroup $H$ then for any $1\ne h\in H$ we have $1\ne \overline{\langle h\rangle^{\mathrm{SS }\le H<G$ so $h$ is a non-trivial non-cyclic automorphism in $G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: For the converse if $1\ne h\in G$ is non-cyclic by definition $\overline{\langle h\rangle^{\mathrm{SS }$ yields a non-trivial proper self-similar closed subgroup of $G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: For self-similar closed subsets the proof is analogous.
elements.sent-2411-128: A self-similar closed subgroup is defined to be cyclically (hypercyclically) generated as a profinite group if it contains a cyclic (hypercyclic) automorphism in the sense of \cref{def: cyclic profinite .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Let us show an explicit example.
elements.sent-2411-131: We define the iterated wreath product $W_H\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ as \begin{equation}W_H:=\{g\in Aut~T~:~ g|_v^1\in H for every v\in T\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}W_H:=\{g\in Aut~T~:~ g|_v^1\in H for every v\in T\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: The set $S:=\bigcup_{n\ge 0 P_n$, where each $P_n$ is the set of $n$-patterns of $W_H$, is countable, so let us write $\{s_i\}_{i\ge 1}$ for the countable set of finitary automorphisms corresponding to each pattern in $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Let us consider the automorphism \begin{equation}g:=\prod_{i\ge 1} s_i*2\overset{i}{\dotsb} 21\in W_H,\end{equation} where $g*v$ stands for the unique automorphism in $\mathrm{St _G(|v|)$ such that $(g*v)|_v=g$ and $(g*v)|_w=1$ for every other vertex $w$ at the same level as $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Then by construction $g|_{2\overset{i {\dotsb 21 =s_i$ for every $i\ge 1$, and therefore \begin{equation}\overline{\{g|_v~:~ v\in T\}}=\overline{\{s_i~:~i\ge 1\}}= W_H\end{equation} yielding that $g$ is hypercyclic.}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We say $g\in G$ is cyclic in~$G$ if $\langle g\rangle^{\mathrm{SS =G$.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: In other words, $g\in G$ is cyclic if there is no proper self-similar subgroup of $G$ containing $g$.
elements.sent-2411-137: The following is proved exactly as \cref{lemma: cyclic vectors in profinite groups :.}
elements.sent-2411-138: A self-similar group $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ contains a non-trivial proper self-similar subgroup if and only if $G$ contains a non-trivial non-cyclic automorphism.}
elements.sent-2411-139: We say that a self-similar discrete group is cyclically generated as a discrete group if it contains a cyclic automorphism in the sense of \cref{def: cyclicity discrete .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Let us return to the example at the beginning of the section.
elements.sent-2411-141: Let $Q\le V$ be the subset consisting of eventually periodic sequences, where an eventually periodic sequence is a sequence $(e_n)_{n\ge 0}$ where there exist $k,N\ge 0$ such that $e_{k+j+nN =e_{k+j}$ for every $n\ge 0$ and $0\le j<N$.}
elements.sent-2411-142: The set $Q$ is a $\tau$-invariant infinite-dimensional dense subspace of $V$ not containing cylic vectors for the left-shift operator $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Furthermore, every vector of $Q$ is contained in a finite $\tau$-invariant subspace.
elements.sent-2411-144: Clearly $Q$ is dense in $A$ as every sequence in $A$ may be approximated by eventually periodic sequences.
elements.sent-2411-145: A linear combination of eventually periodic sequences is again an eventually periodic sequence.
elements.sent-2411-146: Similarly the left-shift of an eventually periodic sequence is again an eventually periodic sequence.
elements.sent-2411-147: Thus $Q$ is a $\tau$-invariant dense subspace of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Finally, the $\tau$-orbit of an eventually periodic sequence is finite, so the $\mathbb{F _p$-subspace which this orbit generates is finite-dimensional over~$\mathbb{F _p$ and thus finite.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Now \cref{proposition: rational subgroup yields the existence of an infinite self-similar discrete group not admitting cyclic automorphisms in the sense of \cref{def: cyclicity discrete :.}}

elements.sent-2411-151: Just consider $G:=f^{-1 (Q)$ by \cref{lemma: self-similar invariant for A and \cref{proposition: rational subgroup .}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Note however that the closure $\overline{G =A\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ admits cyclic and hypercyclic automorphisms in the sense of \cref{def: cyclic profinite by \cref{Theorem: super strongly fractal cyclic , as $\overline{G =A$ is super strongly fractal.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Let us see some examples of self-similar discrete groups acting faithfully on the binary rooted tree and admitting cyclic automorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-154: The first Grigorchuk group $\mathcal{G}$ is generated by the rooted automorphism $a$ corresponding to the cycle $\sigma:=(1\,2)\in \mathrm{Sym (2)$ and the automorphisms $b,c,d$ defined recursively as $\psi(b)=(a,c)$, $\psi(c)=(a,d)$ and $\psi(d)=(1,b)$; see \cite{GrigorchukExample .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Then $\mathcal{G}$ is cyclically generated by any of the $b,c$ and $d$.
elements.sent-2411-156: The Basilica group $\mathcal{B}$ is generated by the automorphisms $\psi(a)=(1,b)\sigma$ and $\psi(b)=(1,a)$; see \cite{Basilica .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Then~$\mathcal{B}$ is cyclically generated by both $a$ and $b$.

elements.sent-2411-163: We devote the remainder of the section to studying cyclicity in automata groups.
elements.sent-2411-164: We recall that an invertible finite-state automaton is a quadruple $(S,X,t,o)$ where:.
elements.sent-2411-165: Finite-state automata are usually represented as labelled directed graphs, where the set of vertices corresponds to the set of states and edges are given by the transition function and labelled according to the output function; see \cref{fig:enter-label below.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Two states are said to be path-connected if there exists a directed path in the automaton from one to the other.
elements.sent-2411-167: A state $s\in S$ is said to be fully connected if there is a path from it to any other state in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-168: If the alphabet $X$ has $m$ symbols we may assume $X=\{1,\dotsc,m\}$.
elements.sent-2411-169: Then the transition and the output functions may be extended to the $m$-adic tree $T$ inductively as \begin{equation}t(xv,s)=t(v,t(x,s))\quadand\quad o(xv,s)=o(x,s)o(v,t(x,s))\end{equation} for $x\in X,s\in S$ and $v\in T$ and both $t(\emptyset,s)=s$ and $o(\emptyset,s)=\emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Therefore, for every state $s\in S$ the function $o_s:T\to T$ given by $o_s(v)=o(v,s)$ for $v\in T$ yields an automorphism of $T$, i.e. $o_s\in \mathrm{Aut ~T$.}
elements.sent-2411-171: A state $s\in S$ is said to be finitary if the corresponding automorphism $o_s$ is finitary.
elements.sent-2411-172: Given a finite-state automaton $(S,X,t,o)$ the subgroup $\langle o_s~:~ s\in S\rangle\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ is the corresponding automata group.}
elements.sent-2411-173: We refer the reader to \cite{Automata for further details on automata groups.}
elements.sent-2411-174: A first observation is that a fully-connected state $s\in S$ yields a cyclic automorphism $o_s\in \mathrm{Aut ~T$.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Let $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ be an automata group given by an automaton $(S,X,t,o)$ containing a fully-connected state $s\in S$.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Then $G$ can be cyclically generated by $o_s$.
elements.sent-2411-177: By definition of fully connected, for each state $s_i\in S$ there exists a vertex $v_i\in T$ such that $o_s|_{v_i =o_{s_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore $\langle o_s\rangle^{\mathrm{SS }\ge \langle o_{s_i}\mid s_i\in S\rangle=G$ and $o_s$ is cyclic.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Let $G$ be a group generated by a finite set $S$.
elements.sent-2411-180: The length of $g$ with respect to~$S$ is defined as the minimal length of a word representing $g$ with letters in $S\cup S^{-1}$, and we denote it by $|g|$.
elements.sent-2411-181: If $G\le \mathrm{Aut ~T$ is self-similar then for $g\in G$ and $n\ge 0$ we define $l_n(g)$ as the maximum of the lengths of the sections of $g$ at the $n$th level of~$T$.}
elements.sent-2411-182: We say $G$ is contracting if there exist constants $C,N\ge 1$ and $\lambda<1$ such that $l_n(g)\le \lambda |g|+C$ for every $n\ge N$ and every $g\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-183: In this case there is a finite set called the contracting nucleus of $G$ such that for any $g\in G$ all the sections of~$g$ deep enough in the tree lie in this contracting nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-184: Now we are in position to state and prove a sufficient condition for cyclicity in contracting automata groups:.

elements.sent-2411-187: If every non-finitary automorphism is cyclic then every finitely generated self-similar proper subgroup is finitary and in particular finite.

elements.sent-2411-195: The aim of this last section is first to study the measure-preserving dynamical systems arising from fractal profinite groups and second to prove hypercyclicity of Haar-random elements in super strongly fractal profinite groups.

elements.sent-2411-238: The first question arising in a measure-preserving dynamical system $(X,\mu,S)$ for a single operator $S$ is how the $S$-iterates of a random point $x\in X$ look like.
elements.sent-2411-239: If $(X,\mu,S)$ is ergodic, then Birkhoff's ergodic theorem yields a nice relation between the time averages and the space averages of the $S$-iterates of any point $x\in X$.
elements.sent-2411-240: Birkhoff's ergodic theorem has been extended from the action of a single operator $S$ to the action of a free group; see \cite{Bufetov, NevoStein .}

elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation}\mu_G(C_A\cap T_v^{-1}(C_B)) =\frac{\# \varphi_\emptyset^{n+m}(C_A\cap T_v^{-1}(C_B))}{|G_{n+m}|} =\frac{\# A}{|G_n|}\cdot \frac{\# B\cdot |St_G(n)\cap \ker \varphi_v^m:St_G(n+m)|}{|St_G(n):St_G(n+m)|} =\frac{\# A}{|G_n|}\cdot \# B\cdot |St_G(n):St_G(n)\cap \ker \varphi_v^m|^{-1} =\mu_G(C_A)\cdot\frac{\#B}{|\varphi_v^m(St_G(n))|} =\mu_G(C_A)\cdot\frac{\#B}{|G_m|} =\mu_G(C_A)\cdot\mu_G(C_B).\qedhere\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-285: The Markov operator $M$ acts on functions and on measures as \begin{equation}(Mf)(x):=\sum_{k=1}^mp_kf(T_kx)\quadand\quad M\mu:=\sum_{k=1}^mp_kT_k\mu,\end{equation} respectively.
elements.sent-2411-286: Furthermore, for $n\ge 1$ and a function $f$ on $X$ we define the $n$th Cesàro average of $f$ as \begin{equation}C_nf:=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{|v|\le n}p_vT_vf,\end{equation} where $p_v$ is the product $\prod_{k=1 ^{|v| p_{x_k}$ given by writing $v=x_1\dotsb x_{|v|}\in T$ with each $x_k\in \{1,\dotsc,m\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: Then for any $1\le p<\infty$ and an arbitrary function $f\in \mathrm{L ^p(X,\mu)$ the Cesàro averages $C_nf$ converge almost continuously to an $M$-invariant function $\widetilde{f}\in \mathrm{L ^p(X,\mu)$ and if $\mu(X)<\infty$, then we have \begin{equation}\int_Xf(x)~d\mu(x)=\int_X\widetilde{f}(x)~d\mu(x).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Therefore by \cref{theorem: Birkhoff theorem the Cesàro averages $C_n \chi_A$ for the uniform distribution $\mathbf{p =\{1/m,\dotsc,1/m\}$ converge almost everywhere to the constant function $\widetilde{f}\equiv \beta$, where \begin{equation}\beta=\frac{1}{\mu_G(G)}\int_G \chi_A(x)~d\mu_G(x)=\mu_G(A)=|G_n|^{-1}>0.\end{equation}.}}
